title: Reusable Design Patterns — Catalog
description: The vocabulary of system design. Each case study in the book reaches for the same recurring patterns; this catalog defines them once — what each is, when to use it, and the trade-off it carries — so the cases can reference them by name. Grouped by the problem they solve.

patternCatalog.name: Horizontal sharding (partitioning)
patternCatalog.what: Split data across nodes by a partition key so no single node holds or serves the whole dataset.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When data volume or write throughput exceeds one node's capacity.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cross-shard operations (joins, transactions) become hard; choosing the key determines everything.

patternCatalog.name: Consistent hashing
patternCatalog.what: Map keys and nodes onto a ring so adding or removing a node only remaps a small fraction of keys.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Distributing keys across a changing set of nodes (caches, shards) with minimal reshuffling.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Uneven load without virtual nodes; rebalancing still moves some data.

patternCatalog.name: Replication
patternCatalog.what: Keep copies of data on multiple nodes for availability, durability, and read scaling.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When you need to survive node loss or serve more reads than one node can.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Replicas lag (eventual consistency) or cost latency (synchronous); read-your-writes needs care.

patternCatalog.name: Quorum reads/writes
patternCatalog.what: Require R reads and W writes such that R + W > N to guarantee overlap with the latest write.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Tuning the consistency/availability trade-off in a replicated store.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Higher R/W means stronger consistency but lower availability and higher latency.

patternCatalog.name: Caching (cache-aside)
patternCatalog.what: Read from cache first; on a miss, read the source of truth and backfill the cache.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Read-heavy workloads with a hot subset of keys where slight staleness is acceptable.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Invalidation is hard; stale reads within the TTL; cold-cache thundering herd.

patternCatalog.name: Read model (CQRS)
patternCatalog.what: Derive a fast, query-optimized projection from the authoritative write store instead of querying it directly.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Read-heavy queries over a normalized or append-only write store that's expensive to aggregate live.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: The read model lags and can have bugs — it must be rebuildable and never the source of truth.

patternCatalog.name: Indexing
patternCatalog.what: Maintain an auxiliary structure (inverted index, B-tree, geo-index) to answer queries without scanning.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Frequent lookups by an attribute that isn't the primary key.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Indexes cost write amplification and storage; stale or missing indexes silently slow queries.

patternCatalog.name: Fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read
patternCatalog.what: Materialize a copy per consumer up front (write) or resolve consumers lazily at read/send time (read).
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Feed and notification delivery; choose by audience size and update frequency.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Write fanout is fast to read but expensive for huge audiences; read fanout is the reverse.

patternCatalog.name: Work queue
patternCatalog.what: Decouple producers from consumers with a durable queue and pull-based workers.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: When producers are fast/bursty but downstream work is slow or rate-limited.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a queue to operate (depth, lag); ordering and exactly-once need extra work.

patternCatalog.name: Transactional outbox
patternCatalog.what: Write events to an outbox table in the same transaction as the state change, then relay them to a queue.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Publishing an event exactly-once alongside a DB write without distributed transactions.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Adds a relay process and outbox cleanup; events are at-least-once, so consumers dedupe.

patternCatalog.name: Saga / state machine
patternCatalog.what: Model a multi-step workflow as explicit states with compensating actions instead of one distributed transaction.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Long-running flows spanning external systems that can fail partway.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Compensation logic is complex; intermediate states are observable and must be handled.

patternCatalog.name: Dead-letter queue
patternCatalog.what: Route messages that exhaust retries to a separate queue for inspection instead of dropping or blocking.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer where some messages are permanently bad (poison messages).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Requires monitoring and a replay/repair process or the DLQ silently grows.

patternCatalog.name: Backpressure
patternCatalog.what: Let a slow consumer signal upstream to slow down (queue growth, rejections) rather than collapse.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any pipeline where producers can outpace consumers.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Someone must absorb the slack (buffer, shed load, or reject); unbounded buffering just defers failure.

patternCatalog.name: Idempotency key
patternCatalog.what: A client-supplied key that lets the server detect and dedupe retries, executing an effect at most once.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any state-changing API over an unreliable network where clients retry — especially money and notifications.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Needs a dedup store with a defined window; clients must generate and reuse the key correctly.

patternCatalog.name: Deduplication
patternCatalog.what: Collapse duplicate events within a window so downstream effects happen once.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: At-least-once pipelines, duplicate user actions, or repeated external events.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Window sizing trades storage against the risk of treating a real second event as a duplicate.

patternCatalog.name: Double-entry ledger
patternCatalog.what: Record every money movement as balanced debit/credit entries that sum to zero, append-only and immutable.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Whenever correctness and auditability of balances matter more than write convenience.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: More writes per transaction; balances must be derived, not stored, which complicates fast reads.

patternCatalog.name: Reconciliation
patternCatalog.what: Periodically compare internal records against an authoritative external source and repair drift.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Any integration where a second system also holds the truth (PSPs, banks, providers).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Break volume grows with scale; needs ops tooling and compensating-correction discipline.

patternCatalog.name: Leases & fencing tokens
patternCatalog.what: Grant time-bounded exclusive access (a lease) and attach a monotonic token so a stale holder is fenced off.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Leader election, distributed locks, single-writer guarantees.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Clock and pause hazards; without fencing tokens a paused holder can act after its lease expired.

patternCatalog.name: Rate limiting / throttling
patternCatalog.what: Cap requests per key (token/leaky bucket) to protect resources and enforce fairness.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Public APIs, multi-tenant systems, and protecting rate-limited downstreams.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Distributed counters need a fast shared store; too strict harms UX, too loose lets abuse through.

patternCatalog.name: Sharded / priority queues
patternCatalog.what: Partition a queue by tenant/key and separate priority lanes so one tenant or bulk load can't starve others.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Multi-tenant async pipelines with mixed urgent and bulk traffic.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: More partitions and lanes to operate; the routing key must actually isolate tenants.

patternCatalog.name: Geo-indexing
patternCatalog.what: Encode locations (geohash, S2, quadtree) so nearby points are queryable by prefix or range.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Proximity search, ride matching, nearby-friends, mapping.
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: Cell-boundary edge cases; hot cells in dense areas need rebalancing.

patternCatalog.name: Provider abstraction / adapter
patternCatalog.what: A uniform interface behind per-provider adapters so downstreams can be added, swapped, or failed over.
patternCatalog.whenToUse: Multiple interchangeable external dependencies (payment PSPs, push/email/SMS vendors).
patternCatalog.tradeoffs: The abstraction can leak provider-specific quirks; health-aware routing adds complexity.

followUps: For a given case, which two or three of these patterns dominate the design, and why?
Which patterns compose well together (e.g. outbox + dedup + work queue), and which conflict?
For each pattern, what is the single failure mode an interviewer most wants you to name?

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
links.why: A practical baseline for availability, reliability, operations, and scaling tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
links.why: A broad reference for replication, partitioning, logs, transactions, and stream processing tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Handling Overload
links.why: Useful for admission control, load shedding, quotas, and graceful degradation under spikes.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: The Tail at Scale
links.why: Explains why p99 latency dominates large fanout systems and motivates hedging, caching, and bounded work.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
links.why: Canonical production design for partitioning, replication, hinted handoff, vector clocks, and tunable consistency.

links.groupDescription: Messaging, event streaming, delivery semantics, and async processing references.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains Kafka's log, partitions, consumer groups, replication, and operational model.

links.groupDescription: Authentication, authorization, policy, abuse, and identity standards.
links.title: Zanzibar: Google's Consistent, Global Authorization System
links.why: Canonical relationship-based authorization system with consistency tokens and global checks.

links.groupDescription: Metrics, tracing, logs, analytics, data pipelines, warehouses, and orchestration references.
links.title: Prometheus Overview
links.why: Concrete time-series monitoring architecture and data model.
